observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to trace/locate the related documents for unidentified properties to substantiate the reported PPE balances of P29,498,916.44 and thereafter maintain the corresponding PPE ledger cards.

The Municipal Mayor should likewise prioritize the creation of the Inventory Committee headed by the Municipal GSO to conduct the physical inventory of all properties of the LGU, to prepare and reconcile the RPCPPE with the accounting records, and to furnish the Audit Team with the RPCPPE. During the actual count, the type, description, serial number, acquisition date, name/designation of employee accountable, location and condition of the property counted shall be indicated in the Inventory Sheet. Serially pre-numbered inventory tags shall also be pasted to the counted item/property for easy identification.

Further, the Municipal GSO should maintain the required Property Cards per category of PPE. Property Accountability Receipts (PARs) should also be issued for all newly acquired PPE including those counted during physical inventory.

We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office record all purchases to its corresponding inventory account classification and to adjust this account for whatever consumption on the basis of the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) duly supported with the original copy of Requisition Issue Slip (RIS) which should be prepared by the requisitioning office to be submitted weekly or monthly to the Municipal Accountant pursuant to Section 21,VolumeI of NGASManualforLGUs.

b. Year-end balances of reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds (1-03-04-050) and Due to Other Funds (2-03-01-010) remained unreconciled, thereby showing a difference of P175,891.67.

As of December 31, 2016, the balances of the two reciprocal accounts are as follows:
Due from Due to Difference
Other Funds Other Funds
General Fund 304,349.50 33,434.54 270,914.96
SEF 6,406.20 49,242.81 (42,836.61)
Trust Fund - 52,186.68 (52,186.68)
Total 310,755.70 134,864.03 175,891.67

As reciprocal accounts, a debit to Due from Other Funds (1-03-04-050) would require a counterpart credit to Due to Other Funds (2-03-01-010) and vice versa. Hence, their balances should be equal or reconciled at any given date. However, comparison of the two accounts showed a difference of P175,891.67 which could be attributed to the failure of the accounting unit to record and account properly the charges affecting the two accounts. Thus, the inequality or discrepancy in the balances of the Due from Other funds and Due to Other Funds accounts rendered doubtful the reliability of the saidaccounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the accounts Due from Other funds (1-03-04-050) and Due to Other Funds (2-03-01-010) and effect the necessary adjustments in the books to present their correct balances in the financial statements.

c. The receivable account of Due from GOCCs (1-03-03-020) still includes payables to GSIS amounting to P132,361.78 representing arrearages of GSIS contributions for the period CY 2000-2001.

The Notes to Financial Statements for CY 2016 disclosed under Note 5.3 the following:
Due from GOCCs amounting to Php132,361.78 represents the arrearages of
GSIS contributions from CY 2000 to 2001 which is subject for
reconciliation/verification with the GSIS Puerto Princesa City Branch.

It bears emphasis those arrearages of GSIS contributions are obligations/payables to GSIS. Thus, the amount of P132,361.78 should have been correctly recorded under a payable account namely the account Due to GSIS (2-02-01-020). As a result, the receivable account Due from GOCCs was overstated whereas the payable account Due to GSIS was understated by the same amount.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant secure the documents related to GSIS arrearages and make the necessary correcting entry in the books to fairly present the financial statements.

d. The account Animal/Zoological Supplies Inventory (1-04-04-040) still includes the cost of goats acquired totaling P106,190.00 despite their distribution or dispersal to proponents.

Note 6 of the Notes to Financial Statements for CY 2016disclosedthat:
Animal/Zoological Supplies Inventory. – is a project from the Department of
Agriculture on goat dispersal under the Comprehensive Livelihood Emergency
Employment Program (CLEEP) which was distributed to proponents of the
project. The project is under the supervision of the Municipal Agriculture office.
(Emphasis supplied)

The above disclosure, which was the same disclosure in previous years, clearly stated that the goats were already distributed or released to the projects proponents as of reporting date. As such, the inventory account was overstated by P106,190.00.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Agriculturist to secure and submit the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) between the Municipality and the Department of Agriculture for the CLEEP goat dispersal project as well as the distribution list duly signed by recipients/proponents.

The Municipal Accountant should verify from the MOA whether the fund shall be applied under a repayment scheme to properly record the distribution in the books as loans receivable from recipients/proponents.

e. The account Other Prepayments (1-05-01-990) which comprises receivables from former LGU officials totaling P301,509.22 could not be verified or substantiated due to absence of subsidiary ledger and related documents.

It was disclosed in Note 7 of the Notes to Financial Statements that the account Other Prepayments (1-03-06-990) represents various claims of former LGU officials who are no longer in the service amounting to P208,513.95 under the General Fund and P92,995.27 under the Special Education Fund.

However, the audit team could not substantiate the amount of P301,509.22 and verify the nature of the transactions due to the absence of subsidiary ledger and the related disbursement vouchers and other supporting documents. Granting they were unliquidated cash advances, the amount should have been correctly recorded as receivable under the account Due from Officers and Employee.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace/locate the related disbursement vouchers and other supporting documents for claims totaling P301,509.22. If details of claims were identified, an accounting entry should be drawn to reclassify the amount to receivable or other appropriate account to fairly present the financial statements.

f. The account Other Payables under the General Fund reflected a negative balance of P335,477.10 as of December 31, 2016 due to over-remittance of the LGU employee’s livelihood loan amortization to the Land Bank of the Philippines.

Note 10 of the Notes to Financial Statements disclosed that the account Other Payables includes the following:
The negative amount on Livelihood Loan of LGU officials and employees from
LBP represents over-remittance which is subject for reconciliation/verification
with loan proponents. (Emphasis supplied)

As pointed out in last year’s audit, the negative balance was unusual since the normal balance of the account Other Payables is a credit balance. Thus, over-remittance of loan amortization to the Land Bank of the Philippines (LBP) would result in a receivable from the said bank and therefore, should require a reclassification of entry from payable to receivable account equivalent to the amount of overpayment.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant verify the amounts of livelihood loan deducted from payrolls, the amount recorded as Other Payables, and the payments made during CY 2016 and prior years to determine the reason for the incurrence of negative balance. In case of overpayment, an entry should be made to reclassify the negative balance to appropriate receivable account.

We recommended that the OIC -Municipal Accountant to:
a. Provide the audit team the supporting documents as a basis for the recording of Prior Period Adjustment/Error;
b. Identify the nature of adjustment/error to determine the extent of the error if it requires prospective or retrospective restatement of financial statement; and
c. Strictly comply with the provisions provided under paragraph 54, PPSAS 3 on the Accounting Policies, Changes in Accounting Estimates and Errors by providing adequate disclosure on the Notes to Financial Statement.

We recommended that the:
a. OIC – Municipal Accountant (i) ensure that the recording of check collections should be properly recorded and accounted before deposit to AGDB, (ii) provide adjusting entries in the books for the unrecognized revenue and understatement of Cash – Local Treasury account and (iii) strictly comply with accounting guidelines as prescribed by the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs); and
b. OIC – Municipal Treasurer (i) ensure that all check collections should be completely reported in the daily collections and deposits with the duplicate of OR attached to the report, (ii) investigate the reason as to why such practice happened and (iii) devise systematic measure in order not to delay the submission of all reports in the Accounting Office for timely recording and reporting.

We recommended that OIC – Municipal Treasurer (i) strictly observe the general principles that all payment should be made by check except it is impossible or impractical to do so; (ii) stop the practice of transferring cash advance to another accountable officer; and (iii) observe strict compliance in all the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Treasurer should apply strict implementation of controls over collections and remittances to avoid misappropriation and/or concealment of irregularities in handling of cash. Collectors/tellers should religiously observe daily remittance of their collections, together with the issued official receipts, and preparation and submission of the corresponding RCDs to the treasurer or cashier to whom the cash collected should be turned over.

b. The ICO-Municipal Treasurer did not prepare RCDs to consolidate remittances of collectors to the Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB), contrary to Section 35 of the said manual.

We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Treasurer should prepare the RCD consolidating the remitted collections supported with the RCDs of collectors, official receipts and validated deposit slips in compliance with Section 35 of the said manual. Furthermore, deposit all collections promptly and intact with the authorized government depository bank.

c. Segregation of duties and functions of accountable officers were not observed in the handling of government funds contrary to Section 50 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual (GAAM), Volume III which may result to possible irregularities and its concealment.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor should designate a disbursing officer who shall exclusively perform the disbursing functions pursuant to Section 3.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Section 50 of GAAM, Volume III. The designated employee should not act as collector at the same time to avoid possible irregularities and its concealment.

We recommended for the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to:
a. Deposit all collections promptly and intact with the authorized government depository bank, in compliance with Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 32 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I to avoid possible loss and misuse of funds; and
b. Immediately discontinue the practice of retaining on hand part of collections and using it to pay miscellaneous expenses. All cash payments must be made out of the cash advance of the Disbursing Officer.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure (i) that all officers and employees who perform duties requiring control, custody or possession of government funds or property be properly bonded; (ii) that the maximum amount of cash accountability based on the approved fidelity bond does not exceed the actual cash accountability of all accountable officers; and (iii) observe strict compliance to Section 305 (f) of Republic Act No. 7160 or the Local Government Code of the Philippines; Section 101 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 or the Government Auditing Code of the Philippines and the provisions of Treasury Circular No. 2-2009 dated August 6, 2009.

We recommend that the OIC – Municipal Accountant to (i) indicate on the face of the DV the list of all supporting documents attached; (ii) follow the guidelines prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2014 to serve as a guide for the complete documentary requirements for common government transactions; and (iii) properly observe the proper preparation of the all accounting forms and reports in accordance with the instructions provided under Chapter 2, Accounting Forms and Reports, Volume II of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs).

Given the findings described above, we recommended to improve the programs and projects implementation of the Municipality and to help ensure that the resources are managed in the most effective, efficient and economical manner to meet the needs of the constituents of the Municipality.

a. To improve planning and budgeting capabilities
i. Carefully plan and organize the project management plan. Often, project planning is ignored in favor of getting on with the work. However, many people fail to realize the value of a project plan in saving time, money and possible problems.
ii. Prepare APPs using the prescribed form as per GPPB Circular No. 07-2015 and ensure that are duly linked to AIP to ensure that all procurement is cost effective and efficient. Also, ensure the timely submission of the APP to the other agencies concerned such as the DBM and GPPB-TSO.
iii. Enlist the help of other experts for technical knowledge on feasibility studies and project development.
iv. Widen the network for collaboration.
v. The guidelines and policies involving the implementation of programs or project should already been included in the planning and budgeting of these projects, duly approved by the Sangguniang Bayan.

b. To ensure that the plans are carried out and to avoid delay in construction
i. Clearly describe how the project will be managed and controlled in its delivery/execution phase
ii. Fast track the implementation of unimplemented PPAs.
iii. Evaluate whether the programs and projects funded from prior years’ budget are still relevant otherwise revert the fund to other programs and projects which is more needed and applicable upon recommendation of the Local Chief Executive and approval by the Sanggunian concerned (Section 322 of RA No. 7160)
iv. Provide timeline for every program/project is and make sure that the timelines set are realistic and is strictly followed.
v. Close coordination among offices.

c. To enhance accountability
i. The MPDC is advised strictly to monitor and evaluate PPAs (Section 112 of RA 7160) and provide for project support or program maintenance.

d. To improve economic efficiency in infrastructure projects.
i. Conduct feasibility study and prepare detailed engineering should be conducted first before implementing any project.
ii. Follow DPWH D.O. No. 22 dated February 18, 2015 for the Design Plans, Program of Works and Approved Budget Contract with a Detailed Cost Estimates.

We recommended that the BAC should:
i. Strictly comply with the provisions of Section 10 of RA 9184 and its IRR. In addition, follow the provisions prescribed under Sections 53.9, 53.9.1 and Annex “H” of the same RA and GPPB Resolution No. 09-2009 dated November 23, 2009;
ii. Conduct rigid screening of eligibility of the suppliers and contractor. Update the compilation of the supplier, contractors and consultant shortlist.
iii. Perform thorough review and evaluation of the legality of the alternative mode of procurement before resorting to shopping and small value procurement.
iv. Meticulously and judiciously prepare APP and PPMP for the procurement of drugs, medicines and all other supplies to avoid splitting of purchase orders.

b. Procurement were not properly certified as to conformity with the essential drug list in the Philippine National Drug Formulary as required under Executive Order No. 49 disregarding the assurance of the potency and effectivenessof thedrugsprocured.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer or the Requisitioning Officers for the procurement of drugs and medicines be required to certify as to the conformity of procured medicines with the essential drug list as contained in the Philippine National Drug Formulary, to ensure delivery of effective medicines to the generalpublic.

c. Procurement exceeded its authorized appropriation in the annual budget by P5,751,367.55 contrary to Section 305 of Republic Act No. 7160 otherwise known as the Local Government Code of the Philippines.

We recommended that the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer to strictly monitor the appropriations for all the objects of expenditures to avoid incurring obligations in excess of the authorized appropriation as mandated by Section 305 (a) of RA 7160 otherwise the excess amount may subject to audit disallowance.

d. The Disbursement Vouchers (DV) and other supporting documents were not properly signed and prepared contrary to the provisions of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445, Republic Act (RA) No. 7160 and COA Circular No. 2006-002 dated January 31, 2006, thus, the validity of the disbursements is doubtful.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, OIC – Municipal Accountant, OIC – Municipal Budget Officer and OIC – Municipal Treasurer to strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 4 of PD 1445, Section 344 of RA 7160 and COA Circular No. 2006-002 dated January 31, 2006. Furthermore, the GSO and BAC Chairman ensure that the Personal Canvass and IAR are duly signed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to instruct all the 34 employees concerned to refund the said amount to the municipal fund. Henceforth, stop the practice of paying appeal fees and other legal expenses of the official or employees using the government funds and strictly adhere to the fundamental principles under Section 4 of the Presidential Decree No. 1445 and Section 305 of Republic Act No. 7160.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive to exert extra effort in finding qualified applicants and appoint a permanent Municipal Accountant that commensurate the qualifications mandated under Article Four, Section 474 of RA No. 7160, to prepare and update the financial reports of the municipal government for the benefit of the end users.

We recommended that management consider filling – up the vacant positions to enhance its services that would give assurance on the attainment of its Mission and Objectives for the benefits of its constituents.

We further recommended prioritizing the creation of additional items (Plantilla or Job Order) under the Municipal Accountant’s Office to ensure that the necessary workforce is obtained to avoid delay in the submission of the monthly, quarterly, and year-end reports that is crucial on the part of the management in decision-making and for the other Government offices concerned.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Officer or Employee concerned to fully comply with the posting requirements on full disclosure as required under DILG MemorandumCircular No. 2013-140 to inform its constituents and the general publicof its accomplishmentsand how publicfundswereutilized.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to cause the immediate submission of the required monthly and quarterly financial reports, SAAOB and transaction documents for CY 2016. Consider the suspension of payment of salaries of officials/employees concerned until they have complied with the requirements of the Commission. Ensure also that succeeding reports, particularly the year-end reports, are submitted on time pursuant to existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that the BAC and General Services Officer (GSO) to ensure the timely submission of copies of government contracts and each of all the documents forming part thereof within five (5) working days from execution pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 to ensure timely review and evaluation of the same so that the management could be immediately notified of any defects/deficiencies noted thereon.

We recommended that the BAC should strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 12.2 by preparing the Procurement Monitoring Report in its prescribed format duly approved by the Municipal Mayor and submitted to the GPPB copy furnish the Audit Team and shall be done on each ensuing semesters thereafter.

We recommended that the OIC - Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the OIC - Municipal Budget Officer, to prepare and submit the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts for CY 2016 and ensure that material variances/differences between budget and actual amounts are properly reviewed, reconciled and adequately disclosed in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer and the MPDC to immediately submit the list of all on-going and to be implemented projects/programs/activities (PPAs) for CY 2016 and succeeding years. Likewise, the monthly monitoring report on implementation of PPAs should also be submitted. Strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 is hereby enjoined.

We recommended that the SWM Board to (i) hasten the preparation of the 10-year SWM Plan for 2016-2025; (ii) see to it that the 10-year SWM Plan is approved by the Palawan Council for Sustainable Development pursuant to R.A. No. 7611 and (iii) ensure the establishment of MRF in every barangay, in accordance with R.A. No. 9003 and its implementing rules and regulations for the furtherance of the objectives of the Republic Act No. 9003.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct that Municipal Environmental and Natural Resources Officer (MENRO) to (i) discontinuing the use of open dump sites and immediately close the dumpsite, (ii) identify possible location for sanitary landfill to develop and operate as a final disposal site for solid wastes. This landfill shall be designed and operated in accordance with the guidelines/criteria mentioned under RA 9003 and (iii) strictly comply with RA 9003, otherwise known as the Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000.

We recommended that the management instruct the Local School Board to convene and submit Annual Local School Board Budget and quarterly utilization reports to ensure that the expenses to be charged in SEF are allowed with proper appropriation and will be accordingly disbursed and monitored.

We recommended the Management to instruct the MDRRMO to furnish the Audit Team with the required Annual LDRRMFIP and the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF for CY 2016, as required under Section 5.1.2 and 5.1.5 of the COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2002 and Section 12 (6) & (7) of the Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction Management Act of 2010 or Republic Act (RA) No. 10121.

Furthermore, ensure that the LDRRMFIP is based on the approved National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan (NDRRMP) 2011-2028, validated and accustomed to the local needs encompassing disaster risk reduction and management such as disaster prevention and mitigation, disaster preparedness, disaster response, and disaster rehabilitation and recovery.

We recommended that the (a) Local Chief Executive instruct the Local Finance Committee(LFC) composed of the OIC - Municipal Treasurer, OIC - Municipal Budget Officer and OIC - Municipal Accountant to explain why the unutilized balances of LDRRMF for current and prior years totaling P27,501,170.87 were not transferred to the Trust Fund; and (b) Municipal Accountant (i) draw a JEV to effect the transfer of such amount from the General Fund to the Trust Fund; and (ii) maintain subsidiary records for the Trust Liabilities-Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund account by sourceandbyyearuponthetransferof cash.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to instruct and remind the Gender and Development Focal Point System (GFPS) on her duty to prepare and submit the Municipal Genderand Development Plans and Budget (GPB) and GAD Accomplishment Report and observe the proper review procedure, in accordance to the abovecited regulations.

Also, for effective planning, budgeting and implementation of GAD Programs and Projects, we recommend that the Municipal GFPS members undergo capacity development trainings in order to strengthen their capabilities in handling the municipal gender and development. They should also be required to establish and maintain the Municipal GAD Database. These undertakings should be considered and included in the preparation of the Municipal GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to cause the immediate submission of
the required monthly and quarterly financial reports, SAAOB and transaction
documents for CY 2016. Consider the suspension of payment of salaries of
officials/employees concerned until they have complied with the requirements of
the Commission. Ensure also that succeeding reports, particularly the year-end
reports, are submitted on time pursuant to existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor compel the Municipal Treasurer to
explain why he should not be criminally and administratively charge for failure
to render accounts of P97,738,565.51. Likewise, submit immediately to the
Municipal Accountant the complete transactions documents and reports
covering January to December 2016. Furthermore, instruct the Municipal
Accountant to prepare and submit to COA the monthly pre-closing trial
balances and quarterly financial statements of each fund covering the same
period. Henceforth, the Mayor should require both the Treasurer and the
Accountant to strictly comply with the submission of year-end reports on or
before February 14 of each year.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:

Require the former ICO-Municipal Treasurer to produce cashbook and to
reconcile with the ICO-Municipal Accountant with their books of accounts
and submit to the Office of the Auditor the report of his accountability in
accordance to Section 80 of PD 1445;

Assign competent personnel to participate in the recenciliation process, exert
effort in tracing up origin of those questionable amounts, if any, identify
those accountable persons responsible for such deviations and assist in
providing pertinent documents to support necessary adjustments in the
books to reflect the correct balances of the account in each fund;

Require all AOs (ICO-Municipal Treasurer, collectors and disbursing
officers) who are handling cash or being granted cash advances to maintain
cashbooks and strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-
002 dated February 10, 1997; Section 26, Volume II of MNGAS for LGUs;
and Section 310 of RA 7160. All AOs should (a) properly maintain their
cashbook by fund; (b) regularly update, balance and reconcile records with
the cash on hand daily or as necessary; and (c) reconcile the cashbooks with
the records of the ICO-Municipal Accountant at least quarterly to ensure
that the recorded transactions are complete, reliable, accurate and timely.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer
and the MPDC to immediately submit the list of all on-going and to be
implemented projects/programs/activities (PPAs) for CY 2016 and succeeding
years. Likewise, the monthly monitoring report on implementation of PPAs
should also be submitted. Strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular
No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 is hereby enjoined.
